title: National Historic Landmarks

infobox.value: (**NHL**) is a [building, district, object, site, or structure](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_property_types "National Register of Historic Places property types") that is officially recognized by the [United States government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") for its outstanding historical significance. Only some 2,500, or roughly three percent, of over 90,000 places listed on the country's [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") (NRHP) are recognized as National Historic Landmarks.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Designation by the US government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Navajo_Nation_Council_Chamber%2C_January_2019.jpg/250px-Navajo_Nation_Council_Chamber%2C_January_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navajo_Nation_Council_Chamber,_January_2019.jpg)

The [Navajo Nation Council Chamber](/wiki/Navajo_Nation_Council_Chamber "Navajo Nation Council Chamber"), the seat of government for [Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") in [Window Rock, Arizona](/wiki/Window_Rock,_Arizona "Window Rock, Arizona"), was declared a National Historic Landmark in 2004.

A **National Historic Landmark** (**NHL**) is a [building, district, object, site, or structure](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_property_types "National Register of Historic Places property types") that is officially recognized by the [United States government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") for its outstanding historical significance. Only some 2,500, or roughly three percent, of over 90,000 places listed on the country's [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") (NRHP) are recognized as National Historic Landmarks.

A [National Historic Landmark District](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark_District "National Historic Landmark District") may include many [contributing properties](/wiki/Contributing_properties "Contributing properties") that are buildings, structures, sites or objects, and it may also include non-contributing properties. Contributing properties may or may not also be separately listed as NHLs or on the NRHP.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Historic_Landmark&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Historic_Landmark&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century")]

The origins of the first National Historic Landmark was a simple cedar post, placed by the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") on their 1804 outbound trek to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") in commemoration of the death from natural causes of Sergeant [Charles Floyd](/wiki/Charles_Floyd_(explorer) "Charles Floyd (explorer)"). The cedar plank was later replaced by a 100 ft (30 m) marble obelisk.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The [Sergeant Floyd Monument](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd_Monument "Sergeant Floyd Monument") in [Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"), was officially designated on June 30, 1960.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Historic_Landmark&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Prior to 1935, efforts to preserve [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage") of national importance were made by piecemeal efforts of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). In 1935, Congress passed the [Historic Sites Act](/wiki/Historic_Sites_Act "Historic Sites Act"), which authorized the [interior secretary](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") authority to formally record and organize historic properties, and to designate properties as having "national historical significance", and gave the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") authority to administer historically significant federally owned properties.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Over the following decades, surveys such as the [Historic American Buildings Survey](/wiki/Historic_American_Buildings_Survey "Historic American Buildings Survey") amassed information about culturally and architecturally significant properties in a program known as the Historic Sites Survey.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Most of the [designations](/wiki/Designation_(heritage_assets) "Designation (heritage assets)") made under this legislation became [National Historic Sites](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_(United_States) "National Historic Sites (United States)"), although the first designation, made December 20, 1935, was for a [National Memorial](/wiki/List_of_National_Memorials_of_the_United_States "List of National Memorials of the United States"), the [Gateway Arch National Park](/wiki/Gateway_Arch_National_Park "Gateway Arch National Park"), then known as the Jefferson National Expansion Memorial, in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri. The first National Historic Site designation was made for the [Salem Maritime National Historic Site](/wiki/Salem_Maritime_National_Historic_Site "Salem Maritime National Historic Site") on March 17, 1938.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Tucson05_TitanICBM.jpg/250px-Tucson05_TitanICBM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tucson05_TitanICBM.jpg)

The [Titan Missile Museum](/wiki/Titan_Missile_Museum "Titan Missile Museum") in [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"), a National Historic Landmark

In 1960, the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") began administering the survey data gathered under this legislation, and the National Historic Landmark program began to take more formal shape.[[5]](#cite_note-5) When the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") was established in 1966, the National Historic Landmark program was encompassed within it, and rules and procedures for inclusion and designation were formalized. Because listings (either on the National Register, or as an NHL) often triggered local preservation laws, legislation in 1980 amended the listing procedures to require owner agreement to the designations.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

On October 9, 1960, 92 places, properties, or districts were announced as eligible to be designated NHLs by [U.S. Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") [Fred A. Seaton](/wiki/Fred_A._Seaton "Fred A. Seaton"). Agreements of owners or responsible parties were subsequently obtained, but all 92 have since been considered listed on that 1960 date.[[note 1]](#cite_note-7)

## Criteria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Historic_Landmark&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Criteria")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Global_Citizen_Festival_Central_Park_New_York_City_from_NYonAir_%2815351915006%29.jpg/250px-Global_Citizen_Festival_Central_Park_New_York_City_from_NYonAir_%2815351915006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Global_Citizen_Festival_Central_Park_New_York_City_from_NYonAir_(15351915006).jpg)

[Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), a prominent National Historic Landmark; New York City has [116 NHLs](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_New_York_City "List of National Historic Landmarks in New York City"), more than any other city in the U.S.

NHLs are designated by the United States secretary of the interior because they are:[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

* Sites where events of national historical significance occurred;
* Places where prominent persons lived or worked;
* Icons of ideals that shaped the nation;
* Outstanding examples of design or construction;
* Places characterizing a way of life; or
* Archeological sites able to yield information.

## Current National Historic Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Historic_Landmark&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Current National Historic Landmarks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Museo_del_Antiguo_Legado_Estadounidense%2C_T%C3%A1nger%2C_Marruecos%2C_2015-12-11%2C_DD_44-46_HDR.JPG/250px-Museo_del_Antiguo_Legado_Estadounidense%2C_T%C3%A1nger%2C_Marruecos%2C_2015-12-11%2C_DD_44-46_HDR.JPG)](/wiki/File:Museo_del_Antiguo_Legado_Estadounidense,_T%C3%A1nger,_Marruecos,_2015-12-11,_DD_44-46_HDR.JPG)

The [American Legation in Tangier](/wiki/American_Legation,_Tangier "American Legation, Tangier") in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), the first National Historic Landmark on foreign soil

[More than 2,500 NHLs](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_by_state "List of National Historic Landmarks by state") have been designated. Most, but not all, are in the United States. There are NHLs in all 50 states and the national capital of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") Three states ([Pennsylvania](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Pennsylvania "List of National Historic Landmarks in Pennsylvania"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Massachusetts "List of National Historic Landmarks in Massachusetts"), and [New York](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_New_York "List of National Historic Landmarks in New York")) account for nearly 25 percent of the nation's NHLs. Each of three cities within these states, [Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Philadelphia "List of National Historic Landmarks in Philadelphia"), [Boston](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Boston "List of National Historic Landmarks in Boston"), and [New York City](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_New_York_City "List of National Historic Landmarks in New York City"), has by itself more NHLs than 40 of the 50 states. New York City alone has more NHLs than all but five states: [Virginia](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Virginia "List of National Historic Landmarks in Virginia"), [California](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_California "List of National Historic Landmarks in California"), Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, and New York, the latter of which has the most NHLs of all 50 states. There are 74 NHLs in the [District of Columbia](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Washington,_D.C. "List of National Historic Landmarks in Washington, D.C.").

Some NHLs are in [U.S. commonwealths and territories, associated states, and foreign states](/wiki/List_of_United_States_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_United_States_commonwealths_and_territories,_associated_states,_and_foreign_states "List of United States National Historic Landmarks in United States commonwealths and territories, associated states, and foreign states"). There are 15 in [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), the [Virgin Islands](/wiki/Virgin_Islands "Virgin Islands"), and other [U.S. commonwealths and territories](/wiki/Commonwealth_(U.S._state) "Commonwealth (U.S. state)"); five in U.S.-associated states such as [Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia"); and [one](/wiki/American_Legation,_Tangier "American Legation, Tangier") in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco").[[9]](#cite_note-NHL_list-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

[Over 100 ships or shipwrecks](/wiki/List_of_U.S._National_Historic_Landmark_ships_and_shipwrecks "List of U.S. National Historic Landmark ships and shipwrecks") have been designated as NHLs.

Approximately half of the National Historic Landmarks are [privately owned](/wiki/Property "Property").[[11]](#cite_note-12) The National Historic Landmarks Program relies on suggestions for new designations from the National Park Service, which also assists in maintaining the [landmarks](/wiki/Landmarks "Landmarks"). A friends' group of owners and managers, the National Historic Landmark Stewards Association, works to preserve, protect and promote National Historic Landmarks.

If not already listed on the National Register of Historic Places, an NHL is automatically added to the Register upon designation; about three percent of Register listings are NHLs. [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Washington,_D.C. "List of National Historic Landmarks in Washington, D.C.") is home to three specifically legislated exceptions to this rule: the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol"), and the [United States Supreme Court Building](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court_Building "United States Supreme Court Building"). All are designated as NHLs, but are not on the National Register.[[12]](#cite_note-13)
